title: Free Speech Protection Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
plain_summary: The Free Speech Protection Act is a new law that stops government workers from telling social media companies like Facebook or Twitter to remove posts or ban users. The law says government agencies have to report when they talk to these companies about what people post online. If someone thinks the government made a social media company unfairly remove their post or block their account, they can take the government to court. This law is meant to protect people's right to free speech on the internet.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management
Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents
plain_keywords: government telling social media what to delete

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Free Speech Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these words have these meanings. Covered information means information about phone calls, digital messages like posts on websites, emails, texts and direct messages, photos, what people buy and where they shop, location data including driving routes and ride sharing information, computer addresses called IP addresses, data about data called metadata, search history, names and ages and other personal information about people who use websites, and calendar items. Covered platform means websites and services where people can interact with each other online, as defined in another federal law about internet services, and any way that news organizations share information whether through TV, radio, print, online, or any other method. Director means the person who runs the Office of Management and Budget. Employee means someone who works for a federal government agency and includes people working under contracts with federal agencies, and also includes the President and Vice President. For contractors, the agency they have a contract with counts as their employer for this law. Executive agency means federal government departments and offices as defined in federal law, and includes the President's office. Provider means a company or organization that operates a covered platform.

sections.title: Section 3 Findings
sections.text: Congress finds these facts. The First Amendment to the Constitution guarantees freedoms about religion, speaking your mind, gathering together, and asking the government to fix problems. It protects free speech by stopping the government from limiting the press or a person's right to speak freely. It gives people the right to gather peacefully and ask the government for help. Free speech is essential for liberty and stops tyranny while empowering people. Thomas Jefferson wrote that there are rights that are useless to give up to the government, and governments always want to take away. These include the rights of thinking and sharing our thoughts by speaking or writing. The Supreme Court has said the right to speak without government interference is a fundamental right. In a case called Palko versus Connecticut in nineteen thirty seven, the Court wrote that freedom of thought and speech is the foundation and necessary condition for almost every other kind of freedom. In Turner Broadcasting System versus Federal Communications Commission in nineteen ninety four, the Court said that the heart of the First Amendment is that each person should decide for themselves what ideas and beliefs deserve to be expressed, considered, and followed. Our political system and culture rest on this idea. Government action that stops speech because of its message, or requires saying a particular message the government likes, goes against this essential right and creates the risk that government wants to suppress unpopular ideas or control public debate through force rather than persuasion. These restrictions raise the fear that government may effectively drive certain ideas or viewpoints from the marketplace. For these reasons, the First Amendment, with only narrow and well understood exceptions, does not allow government control over the content of messages expressed by private individuals. In a case called R.A.V. versus City of St. Paul in nineteen ninety two, the Court explained that the First Amendment generally prevents government from banning speech, or even expressive conduct, because they disapprove of the ideas expressed. Restrictions based on content are presumed to be invalid. The case Brandenburg versus Ohio from nineteen sixty nine stands for the idea that speech can only be suppressed if the speech is intended and likely to produce immediate lawless action. Justice William Brennan, in his majority opinion in Texas versus Johnson in nineteen eighty nine, said that if there is a fundamental principle underlying the First Amendment, it is that government may not prohibit expressing an idea simply because society finds the idea offensive or disagreeable. Justice Neil Gorsuch, in his majority opinion in 303 Creative LLC versus Elenis in twenty twenty three, stated that the First Amendment sees the United States as a rich and complex place where all people are free to think and speak as they wish, not as the government demands. As shown by disclosures from various social media companies, federal officials in recent years have tried to censor legal speech on platforms operated by those companies by using the power of their offices to influence what opinions, views, and other content users of those platforms may share. White House officials and officials of federal agencies tried to silence stories on social media platforms about issues related to the COVID nineteen pandemic. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention worked with officials at Facebook and Twitter to request that certain posts be marked as disinformation and held regular meetings with those companies to share examples of what government officials decided was misinformation about the COVID nineteen pandemic that had been spread on the platforms operated by those companies. During the twenty twenty election, the Federal Bureau of Investigation communicated with high level technology company executives and suggested that a New York Post story about the contents of Hunter Biden's laptop were part of a hack and leak operation. On April twenty seventh, twenty twenty two, the Department of Homeland Security announced the creation of a Disinformation Governance Board. The Director of the Board, Nina Jankowicz, tried to establish an analytic exchange with industry partners. In congressional testimony, Secretary of Homeland Security Alejandro Mayorkas provided misleading testimony about the actions of the Board. Since twenty twenty, two nonprofit organizations connected to the Global Disinformation Index have received a total of three hundred thirty thousand dollars in grants from federal agencies. The Global Disinformation Index maintains a list of global news publications rated high risk for disinformation. Major advertising companies seek guidance from this supposedly nonpartisan group to determine where advertising money should be spent. Despite claiming to be nonpartisan, the Global Disinformation Index includes many reputable media outlets, such as Reason, RealClearPolitics, and the New York Post.

sections.title: Section 4 Employee Prohibitions
sections.text: Federal employees acting under official authority or influence may not use any form of communication, whether the public can see it or not, to direct, force, pressure, or encourage a platform provider to take action, suggest or hint that a provider should take action, or request that a provider take any action to censor speech that is protected by the Constitution. This includes removing that speech from the platform, suppressing that speech on the platform, removing or suspending a particular user or group of users from the platform or limiting their access, labeling that speech as disinformation, misinformation, or false, or making any similar description of the speech, or otherwise blocking, banning, deleting, reducing priority, removing money making ability, reducing reach, or restricting access to the speech. Employees also may not direct or encourage a provider to share with a federal agency covered information containing data about a particular topic, or a user or group of users on the platform, including any covered information shared or stored by users on the platform. Employees may not work, directly or indirectly, with any private or public organization or person to take an action that is prohibited. On behalf of the federal agency employing the employee, employees may not enter into a partnership with a provider to monitor any content shared on the platform or ask for, accept, or enter into a contract or other agreement including a no cost agreement for free advertising or another promotion on a covered platform. The prohibition about sharing covered information does not apply when a federal agency or employee acts according to a warrant issued by a federal court with proper authority following the procedures in rule forty one of the Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure, or by a state court with proper authority. If an employee violates any of these rules, despite any other federal law, the head of the federal agency employing that employee, or the President if the head of an agency violates the rules, must impose disciplinary action consisting of removal, reduction in grade, suspension, or preventing the employee from working for the United States, a civil penalty of at least ten thousand dollars, making the employee ineligible for any retirement benefits under federal law, and permanently taking away any security clearance held by the employee. For contractors who violate these rules, in addition to other discipline, that employee will be barred from working under any contract with the federal government. A person whose account, content, speech, or other information has been affected by a violation of this section may bring a civil lawsuit in the United States District Court for the District of Columbia for reasonable attorneys' fees, court orders to stop the violation, and actual damages against the federal agency and the employee who committed the violation. In such a lawsuit, there will be a rebuttable presumption against the federal agency or employee if the person bringing the lawsuit shows that the employee communicated with a provider about covered information regarding that person or a statement made by that person on the platform.

sections.title: Section 5 Reporting Requirements
sections.text: No later than ninety days after this law is enacted, and at least once every ninety days after that, the head of each federal agency must submit a report to the Director of the Office of Management and Budget and the leaders of certain congressional committees. The report must disclose, for the time period covered by the report, each communication between a representative of a provider and an employee of that federal agency. This includes any communication that violates the employee prohibitions, but does not include communications about fighting child pornography or exploitation, human trafficking, or illegal drug transportation or transactions. Each report must include the name and job title of each employee and each representative of a provider involved in the communication. If the communication violates the employee prohibitions, the report must include a detailed explanation of the nature of the violation and the date of the violation. No later than five days after the Director receives a report, the Director must collect the report and give it a unique tracking number, and publish the contents of the report and the tracking number on a publicly accessible and searchable website. For any report where an individual is the subject, no later than the end of the business day following the business day the report is submitted, the Director must make a reasonable effort to contact any person or organization directly affected by a violation described in the report to inform them about the report.

sections.title: Section 6 Cybersecurity Infrastructure and Security Agency Report
sections.text: No later than one hundred eighty days after this law is enacted, the Secretary of Homeland Security must submit a report to the Director and the leaders of certain congressional committees. The report must disclose any action by an employee of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency that occurred between November sixteenth, twenty eighteen, and when this law is enacted, and would have violated the employee prohibitions.

sections.title: Section 7 Termination of Disinformation Governance Board
sections.text: The Disinformation Governance Board established by the Department of Homeland Security, if it still exists when this law is enacted, is terminated. No federal money may be used to establish or support the activities of any other organization that is substantially similar to the Disinformation Governance Board that was terminated.

sections.title: Section 8 Prohibition on Misinformation and Disinformation Grants
sections.text: The head of a federal agency may not award a grant related to programming on misinformation or disinformation.

sections.title: Section 9 Grant Terms
sections.text: Anyone who receives a grant from a federal agency on or after this law is enacted must promise to the head of the federal agency that they or anyone they give money to from the grant will not, during the time they have the grant, label any creator of news content, regardless of the format, as a source of misinformation or disinformation. No later than ten days after a federal agency awards a grant, the head of the agency must publish this promise on Grants.gov, or any replacement website. If the head of a federal agency determines that a grant recipient or someone they gave grant money to has broken this promise, that person or organization must pay back the grant money and will not be eligible to receive a grant from the federal agency.

sections.title: Section 10 Presidential War Powers Under the Communications Act of 1934
sections.text: This section changes an existing federal law about communications by removing certain subsections and renumbering the remaining ones. It also makes technical changes to another section of the same law by adding the word and before section two and removing extra text at the end.

sections.title: Section 11 Applicability of FOIA
sections.text: In this section, agency has the same meaning as in federal law about government procedures. Despite any other provision of federal law about public access to government records, when someone requests records from an agency about communication between an employee and a representative of a provider, the request must be granted without regard to any exemption in federal law, except the agency may not release any identifying information of a user of a covered platform without written permission from the user to the agency. The agency may not grant the request if the communication happened because of a warrant as described earlier in this law.
